concepts.name: Domain-aware auto-config via YAML presets
concepts.summary: Extend `auto_configure_df()` with a domain detector that chooses a tuned YAML preset (equipment, people, financial, healthcare, electronics, …) as the starting config, then layers the existing regex/profiler heuristics on top. Fixes brittleness where the generic profiler produced bad blocking/scoring configs for domain-specific data like 401k bulldozer equipment.
reasoning: Root cause of the 401K equipment dedup failure: the current auto_configure_df is purely generic — column-name regex plus data profiling — with no concept of 'this is equipment data, use these matchkeys and these blocking rules'.

Chose a layered approach over pure-preset or pure-heuristic: detect domain, load its YAML preset as a starting point, then let the generic profiler refine on top of it. Preserves the zero-config path for simple cases.

Presets live as YAML packs alongside the existing domain YAML files (`goldenmatch/domains/*.yaml`); each pack gains an `autoconfig_preset` field with matchkeys/blocking/standardization/threshold defaults.
Rejected requiring the user to specify domain explicitly — detection has to be automatic to preserve the 'works out of the box' promise.

concepts.name: Autonomous preflight sample run with auto-downgrade
concepts.summary: Before the real dedupe_df run, invoke a `preflight()` stage that takes a ~5K-row stratified sample, runs it through the full pipeline, measures actual memory / pair count / LLM tokens, projects the full-run cost, and either proceeds or auto-downgrades the config (smaller blocks, cheaper scorer, skip oversize) when projections exceed the safety budget — all without user interaction.
reasoning: Constraint from the user: because GoldenMatch is a library embedded in Python backends, preflight cannot require interactive approval — it has to self-correct.
Chose sample size of 5K rows as the default (1% for very large inputs) — small enough to complete in seconds, large enough to expose skewed blocks and LLM latency.

Preflight returns a `RunPlan` dataclass (with `ResourceProjection`, `SampleStats`, and any `Downgrade` actions applied) rather than mutating global config; preserves clean data flow into the main pipeline.
Preflight is integrated into `dedupe_df()` by default (run_preflight=True) rather than being a separate opt-in step, because the whole point is to prevent the user from skipping it accidentally.

concepts.name: Runtime circuit breakers for memory / cost / time budgets
concepts.summary: Real runs diverge from preflight projections (skewed blocks, LLM latency spikes, memory fragmentation). Add a `CircuitBreaker` polled between blocks in scorer.py and between LLM batches in llm_scorer.py; when a memory/cost/time budget is exceeded it raises `CircuitBreakerError` or takes a `CircuitAction` (skip oversize blocks).
reasoning: Preflight alone is insufficient because Python doesn't fully reclaim memory from prior runs — static projections drift on consecutive invocations.
Chose a poll-between-work-units model rather than a background watchdog thread because it plays nicely with Polars locks and avoids cross-thread Python GIL surprises.
Unified circuit breaker over three separate per-axis breakers (memory, cost, time): one object tracks all three budgets, simplifies the API, and lets users set one policy everywhere.
